text: strangers who show kindness and give attention. Each of these changes can affect how someone interacts with money, technology, and people, and that’s exactly how scammers take advantage of these vulnerabilities. How to Protect Your Loved One (and Their Independence) You don’t have to take over someone’s finances to help keep them safe. A few small steps can make a big difference: Set up automatic payments for essentials like utilities and insurance. Add Trusted Contacts to financial accounts, and your Carefull account, so loved ones can be reached if something seems off. Use financial monitoring tools that send gentle alerts for unusual activity or spending changes. Stay connected. Regular check-ins about money, but also about life, reduce isolation and make scams less likely to succeed. Keep learning. Understanding how brain health and financial health overlap helps families spot the earliest warning signs. At Carefull, we see these changes every day, small behavioral changes that can quickly turn into major risks if no one’s watching. Our platform was designed to notice what families might miss: unusual transactions, repeated behaviors, or signs of cognitive decline that could signal trouble. ‍ Jenny Leight Jenny Leight supports Business Development & Strategy at Carefull, the first financial safety platform designed to protect aging adults and their families from elder fraud, scams, and money mistakes. 3 Steps to Safer Money, Try it Free for 30 Days Step 1 Start your free, no-risk trial Step 2 Connect the accounts and cards you want protected Step 3 Stay alerted to any unusual activity Activate your protection Disclaimer: The information and resources above and within the articles are provided for your convenience through Carefull and should not be considered an endorsement of products, services or information provided, or an assurance of security or privacy provided at the linked site.
